title: Evolution of Coherent Structures in Incompressible Flows
speaker: Francisco Gancedo
abstract: In this talk, we will explore recent developments in the study of coherent structures evolving by incompressible flows. Our focus will be on the behavior of fluid interfaces and vortex filaments. We include the dynamics of gravity Stokes interfaces, the Muskat problem, and vortex filaments in both Navier-Stokes and Euler equations.

proof_stages.label: Gravity-driven Stokes patch model
proof_stages.summary: The speaker sets up two-dimensional incompressible Stokes flow with gravity and density patches, explains the Stokeslet representation and its logarithmic growth/Stokes-paradox obstruction, and states the aim of controlling nondecaying interface dynamics.

proof_stages.label: Periodic Stokeslet cancellation and patch regularity
proof_stages.summary: By imposing one-directional periodicity and using the special gravity/density structure, the problematic growth in the periodic Stokes kernel cancels, yielding velocity estimates and propagation of regularity for patch interfaces.

proof_stages.label: Contour equation and weak linear damping
proof_stages.summary: The patch dynamics are reduced to a contour equation, linearized around the flat stationary interface, and shown to exhibit a very weak Fourier damping or growth depending on the stable versus unstable density ordering.

proof_stages.label: Stable-regime global decay
proof_stages.summary: For small graph perturbations in the stable regime, weighted Sobolev energy estimates and Duhamel bounds exploit the weak damping to prove global solutions and polynomial decay.

proof_stages.label: Unstable exponential growth via analytic reversal
proof_stages.summary: Analytic norms with a shrinking strip of analyticity are controlled in the stable regime, and reversing time converts this control into unstable-regime solutions whose analytic norm grows exponentially up to any prescribed time.

proof_stages.label: Muskat comparison through Darcy law
proof_stages.summary: The Muskat problem is briefly formulated via incompressibility, mass conservation, and Darcy's law, and its flat-interface linearization is compared with Stokes by replacing the weak operator |D|^{-1} with the more singular parabolic operator |D|.

proof_stages.label: Helical Navier-Stokes vortex-filament well-posedness
proof_stages.summary: For three-dimensional Navier-Stokes, the speaker defines vortex-filament initial vorticity as a measure concentrated on a curve, recalls straight-line and short-time heuristics, imposes helical symmetry, and states global uniqueness for arbitrary-amplitude smooth helical filaments via local mild theory and global weak-solution estimates.

proof_stages.label: Euler circular filament and convex-integration solution
proof_stages.summary: For Euler, the singular Biot-Savart velocity motivates binormal-flow asymptotics and the vortex-filament conjecture; the final result constructs weak Euler solutions from a circular filament whose thickening and vertical motion match the Navier-Stokes filament heuristic, using convex integration.

examples.name: Ink drop settling in water
examples.note: The ink-in-water picture is a physical visualization of a gravity-driven interface before the two-dimensional Stokes patch model is written down.
